sentences.text: My name is Isaac Richards.
My birth date is the 23rd of September, 1913, and I was born in Liverpool.
May I just correct one thing?
I'm not actually a survivor.
I was not in any of the concentration camps, except in the capacity of a chaplain to the British forces.
Liverpool is a very large city, a port.
At the time, it was one of the major ports of England.
And Jewishly, there were some 7,000 Jewish people who lived in Liverpool.

There must have been quite a lot of synagogue goers, because there were at least five big synagogues, apart from half a dozen smaller what we call [HEBREW], so that people were very well catered for, religiously.
And there was, of course, a-- a Jewish education, which was usually after school.
There were no Jewish day schools.
The Jewish education was given after the ordinary day school, and attended what was called a cheder.
Well, the normal organizations operated, from the [HEBREW], the [HEBREW], to the helping a poor bride and caring for the poor and the sick, and so on.
All the usual institutions of a Jewish community were there in full sway.
We came from what would be called, by modern standards, a pretty orthodox, and certainly observant.
[INAUDIBLE] and such like things were strictly observed in our home, and I think a great majority of the people at that time were-- the older people, certainly, followed that path of life.
Yes, a very large family, by today's standards.
I am the seventh child out of eight.
At all times, yes.
We indeed, did experience rather rough types of anti-Semitism.
I call it rough because what used to happen was, that coming and going at your cheder, which was, from my home-- we didn't live in a very Jewish area-- it was about a mile and a half, two miles.
And on the way back, we would pass through an area which was a little bit rough.
And very often, we were chased by other youngsters, and unpleasant names called after us.
And usually one of our older brothers would accompany us, in case we were attacked, or anything like that.
There was a lot of that kind of thing amongst the-- the youngsters, who very likely learned it from their parents.
Yes.
No, there were no Jewish schools.
Jewish school was the cheder, which we attended every afternoon for some three hours.
And-- and then I went on to the yeshiva, which was under the leadership of Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], who later became the chief rabbi of Israel.
And-- and I continued my studies there at the yeshiva.

Then I was qualified by the-- the rabbi-- the Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], the chief rabbi, and served as a-- with the title of Reverend at first, in a little place in North Wales called Bangor, a very pretty place, very pretty indeed, and then in a small industrial town-- well, not so small by South African standards-- called Bolton, near Manchester.
That was from about 1937, and first in-- in Bangor for a couple of years, and then in Bolton, until I went-- went into the army.
Well, frankly, I don't think it affected very much.
Despite what I said about the youngsters persecuting us, we found very little anti-Semitism.
Now, my parents, as I said, lived in a completely non-Jewish area.
There weren't many Jewish families.
And I went to a school where, at one time, I was the only Jew in the school.
And every consideration was given.
And particularly, I remember, as Passover approaches, that we always used to take some homemade wine and some matzah and give it to the teachers.
And the principal would always come and personally visit to thank us, and get an extra bottle of wine.
Effectively, there was no--
Quite the-- quite the reverse.
We found in our community, that although my mother could hardly speak English, yet she seemed to get on very well with the neighbors.
And even the church people were very kind to us and used to throw business my father's way.
Because he wasn't a great businessman, and with a big family like that, you can well imagine he had quite a struggle.
He was in manufacturing furniture.
But he wasn't a practical man himself, so that was a drawback.
I volunteered as a chaplain, because ministers were excluded from being called up, and-- and I was called up in 1943, early in 1943.
I volunteered a few years earlier.
Yes.
Shortly after the beginning of the war, yes.
After-- well, immediately, one received the rank of captain.
Yes.
No, I don't think so.
People-- we didn't experience very much in Bolton, where I lived.
But from the reports, from bigger cities, where people spent nights and nights in the shelters, no.
I can't recall any major sort of breakouts of anti-Semitism, or anything like that.
Everybody was in trouble together.
Oh, we knew of the Nuremberg laws.

These were reported to us mainly in the Jewish press, the We knew about that, and we knew about Kristallnacht, but we certainly did not know-- did not understand, and hardly could comprehend-- what was going on in the concentration camps and how Jews were being slaughtered.
Yes, I-- I don't think many people knew, outside, maybe, some of the leadership in London.
But many people did not really understand this until the news reels began, on-- particularly the news reels about Bergen-Belsen.
Oh, I doubt it.
Were not known, generally.
No.
No.
I went straight into the chaplaincy.
No.

It's called a chaplaincy corps, and I was stationed at the-- at a place called Colchester, which was the largest intake center for new recruits being brought in from the whole of the London-- of the southern England area.
And at any one time, there were many, many thousands of boys coming there for the-- what you call square bashing, after which they were posted to various units.

That was the basic training camp, and I established a little synagogue on the camp, and we had very-- very-- a very nice arrangement where the boys were free, particularly on Friday night-- sometimes on Saturdays-- and we used to get good gatherings from the recruits who were only too glad to be able to see something of Jewishness, like they were experiencing in their own home.
Fair.
Oh, yes.
Well, everybody had to go, so there was quite a large intake, per capita, of Jewish boys.
Yes, and particularly in London.
No, not the faintest idea.
Yes.
Well, as-- as I said, I was stationed in Colchester.

And shortly after the Bergen-Belsen camp had been liberated, there was a dire need for at least one or two men, who had some authority, who could represent the displaced persons, as they were then called, who could care for them, could act as liaison for them, with the British forces.
And so, I and another Jewish chaplain were posted immediately-- we had about a week's notice-- were posted to Bergen-Belsen direct.
And we took off from London.
On the 8th of May.
VE day, and you can imagine, Piccadilly circus.
It was a real circus.
And we had to report to a little office there, and we got on the bus and were taken to the airport and flew out from England.
And we landed in Belgium.
We couldn't get transport on for a day or two, so we spent the evening in Brussels.
And a day or two later, we landed at an airport near to Bergen-Belsen.

We were met by the senior Jewish chaplain, known as Isaac Levy, or Ike Levy, and he took us over to Bergen-Belsen, and, as it were, threw us head-first into what was obviously a tremendously strange experience to us.
And, of course, we witnessed the horror of all that was going on.

Those who survived were-- had barely survived, and for weeks, many hundreds of people were still dying from the effects of starvation, and even some from the effects of eating the wrong food when the [?
Tommies, ?] out of the goodness of their heart, gave them tins of bully beef, and food like that, which the-- what is the word-- their stomachs just couldn't--
Rejected it.
--couldn't-- couldn't take-- take in.
The films, yes.
Seen the-- Oh, yes we'd seen 'em.
Idea of what-- yeah.
Of what we were going to-- but we received no instructions from the senior rabbi, who'd never been there, sort of thing.
We received no instructions, and we had to work out ourselves, what we were-- had to do, what we did.
We were the first to come and to-- I was the first to come and to stay.
There was-- the-- the chaplain who was with the unit that liberated.
On the 15th-- no. 15th of April, yes.
On the 15th of April.
He stayed there a few days, and then, as his unit moved on, he moved on with them.
He was-- yes.
He was--
He was-- one would say he was sort of living with a particular unit, you see, as we later did.
Yes.
He moved on.
Oh, I had an idea of what-- what to see, what-- what we would meet.
Yes.
But how we were to handle it, that was something different.
And we had to work that out for ourselves.
On the-- about the 10th of May.
Yes.
Well, it was still a tremendous shock to see mounds of bodies lying there, to see walking skeletons in striped uniforms that they still were wearing.
And-- and for them-- if I may digress-- for them, it was a strange and wonderful thing to see a-- an army person with the Magen David on his lapels and on his hat.
And as I was not bearded, for a time, they-- they couldn't realize that I was a rabbi, because, to them, a rabbi was somebody with a long-- very long beard, and so on.
But they soon got used to us, and-- and were very grateful for everything that we were able to do for them.
The original Bergen-Belsen camp, as it was, as you saw it on the-- on the films.
Exactly.
Right.
It was a tremendously large camp, and it wasn't only for Jews.
There were lots and lots of-- of non-Jews in the camp, and Jews from various parts of Europe.
Many different parts.
But by the time we got there, many French Jews or Belgian Jews or Holland Jews had begun to be repatriated.
What was left was, in the main, Polish Jews.
Mainly Polish.
Well, because the authorities would have been delighted and wanted to try and return them.
You must go back.
You came from Poland.
You must go back to Poland.
You came from Lithuania.
You must go back to Lithuania.
But the Jews in the camp said, no.
We are not going back to places which are merely burial places, which are merely cemeteries.
We don't-- Warsaw is no longer a Jewish place.
It's just a Jewish cemetery.
And the same with all the small towns of Lithuania and Poland.

And so, the family determined themselves, and there arose their leaders-- we must mention that a bit later-- and the whole policy was to keep them there until such time-- and in this way, to force the hand-- hands of the British authorities to allow them to go to what then was called Palestine.
This is what, in the main--
--these people wanted.
And this was the official policy of the Zionist movement, to keep them together.
And this leads on a little later to something else I want to say.
They knew.
They knew, yes.
And how-- and how they were aware.
They were completely aware of what had happened.
They were part of it.
They were driven out of their hometowns, and they were herded into ghettos.
They were herded into trains, taken to Auschwitz and so on, and then onto Bergen-Belsen.
Bergen-Belsen became overcrowded, because the Russians were advancing so quickly, and the-- and the Germans didn't want the Russians to see what they had done.
And so they pushed thousands and thousands from those camps which were nearer to where the Russians were advancing, and marched them for days and days into Bergen-Belsen.
And this is why it was bursting at the seams.
Yes.
Yeah.
That's right.
Correct.
Well, the task ahead of us, I saw simply is to help them to return to normalcy, to live a reasonably normal life.
And this began to be realized in some very small measure.
First of all, I must say, pay tribute to some of the British authorities, who did outstanding work.
There was a brigadier, I can't remember his name.
I know later he was a great rugby man, because he's been to South Africa a number of times.
He was a medical man, and he established a hospital for those who were in need of treatment.
Now, what happened then was that the old camp, which was ridden with all sort of infectious diseases--
Every time you went in or out, you had to be sprayed with-- with-- I don't know what it was.
White powder to kill the--
Disinfectant.
And-- and they decided to burn down the old camp.
And first, they transferred all the survivors to a nearby Panzer barracks, very, very modern, and with a very nice facility.
Well, it was for them.
I mean--
At least they had a clean room, and they had-- they had certain toilet facilities.
Not in the rooms, of course, but-- but at least it was a tremendous advance on the horror camp in which they had been living, where one on top of the other were-- were defecating and everything else.
Hardly-- hardly any facilities, and the sick were lying there dying, and everything was mixed up.
And so we transferred these people slowly.
And a very interesting facet was that the great majority were women.
Somehow or other, they had been able to withstand more than the men.
And the majority were women, and many of these-- most of them-- had formed little groups with older women and younger women, and even young-- youngsters, teenagers, and-- and formed little families.
And when we were trying to move them, we had to try and move the little families together.
And if you had a room which was allocated for five, and the family was six, then you had a bit of a problem.
It was at this stage-- if I may interpolate-- it was at this stage that I met a man who became a great figure in the world of the displaced persons, named [?
Yossele ?] [?
Rosensaft. ?] [?
Yossele ?] was a little man, Polish Jew, a former, obviously, union organizer.
And he began to help in the organization, not through us, but on his own.

And-- and while I was trying to fit-- I'll never forget-- while I was trying to fit some people into a particular room, he came along and tried to undo what I was doing, and I got hold of him by the scruff of his neck, and told him to get away.
And mind you, he never held it against me, because we became quite friendly afterwards.
And of course, I must mention this, that I wasn't alone.
Where they came from exactly, I received the help of a young Jewish woman who was-- had been with a Quaker team.
And she left her team, which had gone deeper into Germany, and she offered her services to me.
And a Polish-French rabbi who had served with the French-- Polish Army in France and had been a prisoner of war, and he came.
And we formed a kind of a threesome, and we worked together and-- looking after these people.
And so, they were transferred and settled down there.
And [?
Yossele ?] [?
Rosensaft ?] and the other leaders began to emerge.
They started a school.
Even later on, after I'd left, I think, it came to fruition.
They started a theatre.
They had adult classes, and not just for-- for religious.
They-- they-- they survivors were not necessarily religious.
In fact, I think most of them were not terribly religious.
There were the religious groups, but in the main, they were left wing and not particularly interested in religious matters.
Well, I think-- I think because the idea of-- of the land of Israel, now, was the answer to them, after what they had suffered, and were afraid they might suffer in any other country.
They wanted to have a homeland of their own.
I'm sure that was the motivation.
Yes.
I don't think there were--
I think women-- women have got more guts, in some respect, than men.
And-- and they have more feeling-- I don't know-- for the future, or what.
I-- really, I-- I'm not into being able to answer that question.
But as a fact, I think the statistics will show that more women survived.
I don't know that there were many very old women, but more in the 50s downwards.
And not too many very young children, mostly teenagers.
Were sent away, yes.
Well, no.
There, we had no trouble.
They were eager to go.
They saw the place, and they-- they were eager to go.
It wasn't-- it-- it was only a mile, a mile and a quarter away.
I don't remember how we got them there.
I-- many of them might have walked, even.
And so there was little trouble there, over that.
Must have been about three or four weeks.
Oh yes.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah, it was burnt down.
It was quite amusing, actually.
It was ceremonially-- all the brigadiers and the-- and all the big shots came, and they brought flame throwers and-- to burn it down.
But the first two shots missed.
[LAUGHS] Eventually, they managed to get it ignited, and it was burned to the ground.
Nothing was left.
